comments.text: GPT-5.5 Pro + harness proposes a note improving the direct ENS-derived absolute-gap exponent from \(5+2\sqrt6\) to \(3+2\sqrt2\): PDF note. A Lean formalization (Codex + harness) is here: formalized proof. It verifies an infinite admissible \(A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}\subset\mathbb N\) with\[ a_{n+1}-a_n=o\!\left(n^{3+2\sqrt2}\right). \]This is for absolute gaps, not \(a_{n+1}/a_n\to1\).
What was the specific Harness used for Codex? Have you tried using /goal in the codex app?

I think this may be outside the scope of this forum but I used a fairly complex custom harness that I designed for a math-for-physics formalization project. Among other things, it can call GPT-5.5 Pro when needed and backtrack more aggressively than Codex itself. I doubt that was necessary for this simpler formalization but when I compared it with Codex on [1032]'s partial bound formalization (28k lines of Lean), Codex with GPT-5.5 xhigh got stuck several times. /goal is nice but I'm not sure it's really better than queueing "continue working" messages.

Agreed, a little tangent here I suppose. I solely use /goal for when I'm unable to type those messages, and frankly would use a custom harness, but 5.5 pro api costs are too much hah. Best of luck in your endeavors.

Congrats. Standard check found no issues in the notes. However, there is some presentation issue. The Lean only formalizes $a_{n+1} - a_n = o(n^{3+2\sqrt{2}})$ part of Theorem 1, but not the $a_{n+1} - a_n \le n^{3+2\sqrt{2}}$ part. The asymptotic part is arguably more important, but the paper states that the whole Theorem 1 has been formalized. Also note that the terms "ENS" (referring to Erdős–Nicolas–Sárközy) and "absolute gap" (referring to $a_{n+1} - a_n$) are not standard.

Thank you for checking. The formalization is now fixed. Challenge.lean now states the all-index pointwise gap bound and Solution.lean proves it from the new prefixed endpoint rather than from the previous eventual-bound endpoint.

GPT-5.2 Thinking identifies the following additional references: 1. Erdős–Nicolas–Sárközy, Sommes de sous-ensembles (J. Th'eor. Nombres Bordeaux 3 (1991), 55--72) Here, they construct an infinite admissible set with $A(x):=\#\{a\in A:a\le x \} \gg x^{5-2\sqrt6}$ (Thm. 2). In particular this implies polynomial growth $a_n\ll n^{1/(5-2\sqrt6)}$, hence $\liminf_{n\to\infty} a_{n+1}/a_n=1$. So admissible sets of polynomial growth are known in the literature (at least since 1991). 2. Deshouillers--Freiman, On an additive problem of Erdős and Straus, 2 (Astérisque 258 (1999)) Here, they prove the sharp finite extremal bound: for $N$ large, any admissible $A\subset[1,N]$ satisfies $|A|\le 2\sqrt N + O(1)$ (indeed $\le 2\sqrt{N+\tfrac14}-1$). Consequently any infinite admissible $A=\{a_n\}$ has $A(x)\le (2+o(1))\sqrt x$ and $a_n\ge (1+o(1))n^2/4$; so an eventual gap bound $a_{n+1}-a_n\le n^c$ forces $c\ge 1$ (although it is also possible that no such $c$ exists for some choices of $A$).
